ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for linguine with seafood in creamy pink sauce; A00LC (Pasta, cooked) is the nearest defensible parent category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One restaurant serving of linguine with crab and scallops in creamy pink sauce, approximately 380 g, as typically plated at an Italian restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Pasta with seafood composite; supplemented with USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked linguine (NDB 20121), crab meat (NDB 15139), sea scallops (NDB 15171), and heavy cream (NDB 01053)
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for this exact dish. Nutrient values per 100 g are estimated from a weighted composite of cooked linguine (~55% by weight), Hokkaido scallops (~18%), crab meat (~15%), heavy cream (~7%), and tomato-based sauce (~5%), using USDA SR Legacy values for each component. Energy cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central pasta-with-seafood entries. Values represent a plausible central estimate; actual restaurant preparation may vary.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; cooked pasta (linguine/spaghetti, white, boiled) GI reference values
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists for this specific composite dish. GI of 46 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked white pasta (linguine/spaghetti boiled, GI ~44–48, reference glucose = 100). The dish is dominated by linguine (~55% by weight); the cream, crab, and scallop components contribute negligible glycemic load. A GI of 46 (midpoint of the pasta range) is used as the analogous estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (46 × 17.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 380 g serving) / 100 = 30.6, rounded to 30.8 accounting for slight sauce carbohydrate contribution. gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~1178 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a single restaurant serving of this dish may contribute approximately 50% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~31 GL units per serving) from refined white linguine pasta
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should note that the refined white pasta base produces a moderate glycemic load per serving; choosing a smaller portion, pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables, or substituting wholegrain pasta where available can help attenuate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from cream (~2.1 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium per serving
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The creamy pink sauce contributes saturated fat and the dish carries a high per-serving sodium load; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, favour preparations with less cream, and monitor overall daily saturated fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Linguine with Crab and Scallops in Creamy Pink Sauce (Italian: Linguine Granchio e Capesante) is an Italian seafood pasta dish consisting of long, flat linguine noodles served with Hokkaido scallops, crab meat, and a creamy pink sauce made from a blend of tomato and heavy cream, typically finished with garlic, white wine, and olive oil. The dish originates from the Italian coastal pasta tradition and is served as a main course at Italian restaurants. In Singapore, it is offered at establishments such as Ricciotti at The Riverwalk. The texture combines the al dente bite of linguine with the tender, sweet flesh of scallops and the delicate, slightly briny flavour of crab meat, all enveloped in a rich, velvety sauce that balances acidity from tomato with the richness of cream. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate protein (~9 g per 100 g) from the seafood components, moderate carbohydrate (~17.5 g per 100 g) from the pasta, and moderate fat (~4.8 g per 100 g) largely from cream and olive oil. The glycemic index is low (GI ~46) due to the pasta matrix, though the glycemic load per restaurant serving is moderate (~31 GL units). Sodium content is elevated at approximately 310 mg per 100 g, making portion awareness important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is a source of vitamin B12 and selenium from the seafood, and provides iron and phosphorus. It contains gluten (pasta), crustaceans (crab), molluscs (scallops), and dairy (cream), making it unsuitable for individuals with these allergens.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy component entries (cooked linguine, scallops, crab meat, heavy cream). GI is analogously estimated from University of Sydney GI Database pasta values. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the dish composition is well-defined from the menu description.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component entries used for this composite estimate. 2. Trans fat is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for the component ingredients (cooked pasta, scallops, crab meat, cream) — no industrial trans fats are present. 3. Nutrient values are composite estimates; actual values will vary by restaurant preparation, cream quantity, and portion size. 4. The dish is served at Ricciotti - The Riverwalk, Singapore, at SGD 39.24 per serving. 5. GI of 46 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database pasta reference values; gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. 6. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (46 × 17.5 × 380) / 100 / 100 ≈ 30.8 GL units. 7. This is an Italian cuisine dish; multilingual naming rules for Italian cuisine do not mandate Chinese, Malay, Tamil, or Hindi local names — those fields are left null. The romanized Italian name 'Linguine Granchio e Capesante' is preserved in romanized_local and aliases. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are not -1; they are plausible composite estimates. Only vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the source data.
